articles.title: Ancient Snake Fossil Rewrites Evolution: It Had Legs and a Bone Scientists Got Wrong for 160 Years
articles.summary: A 100-million-year-old fossil from Argentina is shaking up what we know about snake evolution. The snake, Najash rionegrina, had hind legs and a cheekbone (jugal bone) — a feature almost completely lost in modern snakes. Scientists used micro-CT scanning to see inside the fossil and discovered that early snakes were likely big-bodied and big-mouthed, not small burrowers as previously believed. This finding corrects a 160-year-old misunderstanding about snake skull anatomy. Pretty wild, right?

articles.title: Adrenaline: The Hormone That Makes Your Heart Race — and Could Save Your Life
articles.summary: Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a hormone that triggers your body's fight-or-flight response. When your brain perceives a threat, it signals your adrenal glands to release adrenaline, which makes your heart beat faster, increases breathing rate, and slows digestion. But adrenaline isn't just for danger — roller coasters, scary movies, and even crushes can trigger it. Too much over time can harm your heart, but deep breathing and talking to friends can help. Adrenaline is also a lifesaving medication used in EpiPens for severe allergic reactions.

articles.title: NASA's New Machine Can Make IV Fluid in Space — No More Expired Supplies on Mars Missions
articles.summary: NASA has developed a machine called IVGEN Mini that can produce IV fluid (sterile saltwater used to treat dehydration, burns, and other medical issues) from space station drinking water. Current prepacked IV fluid expires after 16 months, but a Mars mission could last three years. IVGEN Mini flew to the ISS on April 11, 2026, and will be tested this spring. It's smaller and lighter than its predecessor, producing 1.2 liters per hour. This technology could be crucial for deep-space missions, reducing cargo weight and ensuring fresh supplies.
